papers.title: Decomposition in Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels
papers.journal: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
papers.paper_scope: Study austenite decomposition reactions (discontinuous precipitation, precipitation transformation, cellular transformation, spinodal decomposition) in Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels at 600°C and 800°C tempering, characterize microstructures and Vickers hardness.

processes.description: Cast, hot-rolled at 1150°C to 5 mm flat product, water quenched, then tempered at 600°C for 30 min, water quenched.

structures.overall_structure: Partial discontinuous precipitation: γ grains transformed into γ+κ lamellae starting from grain boundaries; some untransformed γ band-shaped regions remain.

phases_present.morphology: untransformed band-shaped regions
fine lamellae within transformed γ

structures.overall_structure: Almost complete discontinuous precipitation: most γ transformed into γ+κ lamellar structure, possibly with some α phase; few residual γ bands survive and may contain nano-sized κ precipitates.

phases_present.morphology: residual band-shaped regions
fine lamellae in γ+κ lamellar colonies
may coexist with lamellar product

precipitates.distribution: fine lamellae, also nano-sized possible in residual γ

structures.overall_structure: Cellular transformation and spinodal decomposition: cellular transformation produces κ+α+residual-γ lamellar structures; spinodal decomposition gives nano-sized κ in untransformed γ grains.

phases_present.morphology: residual in cellular regions, spinodal decomposed grains
lamellar with κ, cellular product
lamellar in cellular zones; nano-sized acicular agglomerates in spinodal zones

structures.overall_structure: Whole γ volume undergoes multifaceted precipitation transformation: austenite matrix with carbon-depleted γ regions and globular κ-carbides; strong κ precipitation along δ/γ boundaries and some κ within ferrite.

phases_present.morphology: matrix with carbon-depleted zones
globular carbides in carbon-depleted γ and along δ/γ boundaries
ferrite grains, some κ precipitation inside

precipitates.distribution: at δ/γ boundaries and in carbon-depleted γ

structures.overall_structure: Precipitation transformation: γ+κ lamellae and alternating bands of two different γ grades (one carbon-depleted with globular κ). No lamellar structure like discontinuous precipitation; globular κ embedded in matrix.

phases_present.morphology: two grades: carbon-depleted with globular κ and other grade
globular carbides in carbon-depleted γ bands

structures.overall_structure: Complete cellular transformation: α+κ coarse lamellae (lath-like κ strips) with wide inter-lamellar spacing; scattered thin band-shaped δ-ferrite grains.

phases_present.morphology: matrix of ferrite lamellae
wide, thick lath-like strips in α+κ lamellae

precipitates.distribution: in α+κ lamellar colonies, wide inter-lamellar spacing

structures.overall_structure: Full austenitic decomposition: cellular transformation into κ+α+residual-γ lamellar structure, plus spinodal decomposition in other γ grains; κ appears as wide, thick lath-like lamellae; mixed structure with δ ferrite branches extending into spinodal decomposed γ.

phases_present.morphology: ferrite lamellae in cellular regions; δ-ferrite bands
residual γ in cellular lamellae; spinodal decomposed γ grains
wide, thick lath-like strips in cellular product; cuboidal nano-sized precipitates in spinodal zones

structures.overall_structure: Fully spinodal decomposed austenite: nano-sized κ-carbides within γ grains; morphology: acicular-like, fine and less dense.

phases_present.morphology: matrix, spinodal decomposed
nano-sized acicular agglomerates, fine, less dense

structures.overall_structure: No austenite transformation detected; secondary phases along grain boundaries (possibly γ+α in 9Al-20Mn-1C and only κ in 9Al-30Mn-1C); austenite grains show parallel striations with different orientations.

phases_present.morphology: grains with parallel striations
along grain boundaries, possible continuity with δ

precipitates.type: γ+α or κ at grain boundaries

structures.overall_structure: Similar to 9Al-20Mn-1C-600: no austenite transformation, but only κ-carbides at grain boundaries; austenite grains with parallel striations.

phases_present.morphology: grains with parallel striations
carbides along grain boundaries

interface_notes.slippage_situation: Discontinuous precipitation starts from γ grain boundaries, leading to lamellar γ+κ growth.

interfaces.phase_evolution: γ transforms to γ+κ lamellae at grain boundaries.

interface_notes.slippage_situation: Lamellar product formed by grain boundary migration (discontinuous precipitation).

interfaces.phase_evolution: Nearly complete γ transformation to γ+κ lamellae.

interface_notes.slippage_situation: Cellular transformation produces α+κ+γ lamellae; spinodal decomposition forms nano κ within γ.
Strong κ precipitation along δ/γ boundaries; globular κ in carbon-depleted γ.

interfaces.phase_evolution: Precipitation transformation with κ at interfaces.

interface_notes.slippage_situation: Coarse lamellae with wide inter-lamellar spacing.
δ ferrite branches extend into spinodal decomposed γ.

interfaces.phase_evolution: Cellular transformation with spinodal decomposition, mixed interface.

characterization_methods.purpose: Microstructure observation of all alloys at different processing conditions
characterization_methods.key_findings: Revealed austenite decomposition products such as lamellar structures, phase distributions, and grain boundary features.
Imaged lamellar structures, nano-sized κ-carbides, and celluar/spinodal features (Figs. 2,5,6,7,8).

characterization_methods.purpose: Qualitative local chemical composition of phases
characterization_methods.key_findings: Table 3: Al and Mn distribution in δ, γ, κ and γ nano-κ regions; Mn enrichment in nano-κ region (mean Mn 13.4 wt%).
Table 6: Al and Mn contents in δ, γ, κ; κ shows higher Al (mean 9.4 wt%) and Mn (mean 12.3 wt%) compared to γ.
Table 7: Al and Mn in δ, γ, κ; κ enriched in Al (10.3-11.9 wt%) and Mn (21.9-25.5 wt%).

characterization_methods.purpose: Measure hardness, 5 kg load, 5 indentations per sample
characterization_methods.key_findings: Hardness values reported in Tables 2,4,5,8,9; correlated with microstructure (discontinuous precipitation, cellular transformation, spinodal decomposition).

characterization_methods.purpose: Assess thermodynamic stability of phases at different temperatures
characterization_methods.key_findings: Figures 9 and 12 show phase fractions vs. temperature; used to explain austenite transformation driving forces.

computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Qualitatively consistent with observed austenite decomposition trends.

analytical_model.equation_role: predict phase stability and fraction versus temperature
analytical_model.model_note: Used to explain why austenite transforms at certain tempering temperatures based on driving force.

unmapped_findings: Inter-lamellar spacing influences hardness: fine lamellae with narrow spacing (e.g., 9Al-10Mn-1C at 600°C) give higher hardness (439 HV) compared to coarse lamellae with wide spacing (e.g., 12Al-10Mn-1C at 800°C, 399 HV).
Spinodal decomposition produces different nano-κ morphologies depending on composition: acicular agglomerated (9Al-10Mn-1C), dense cuboidal (12Al-20Mn-1C), fine acicular less dense (12Al-30Mn-1C).
In 9Al-20Mn-1C and 9Al-30Mn-1C at 600°C, no austenite transformation was observed; hardness increase attributed possibly to solid solution strengthening or very fine nano-κ.
Austenite stability is influenced by Mn content and tempering temperature: higher Mn stabilizes γ, requiring spinodal decomposition over cellular transformation at 800°C.
Discontinuous precipitation occurs at 600°C in medium Mn (10%), high Al (9%), high C (1%) combinations; requires grain boundary migration.
Cellular transformation developed at 800°C between 9-12% Al; spinodal decomposition becomes dominant at higher Mn (30%) and 1% C due to balanced driving force.
Hot-rolled microstructure not characterized in detail; only hardness values provided for comparison.
Optical micrographs in Fig. 5 show mixed structures of cellular and spinodal decomposition; SEM images show nano-κ details.
Table 9 compares inter-lamellar spacing and hardness for 12Al-10Mn-1C 800°C (wide spacing) and 9Al-10Mn-1C 600°C (narrow spacing).
The paper suggests the best balance of hardness and drawability is achieved in 9Al-10Mn-1C at 600°C via discontinuous precipitation with fine lamellae.
